APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a new sequence-labeling architecture (ID-CNN) and evaluates on standard NER benchmarks (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes 5.0) using segment-level micro-averaged F1. The paper compares against appropriate strong baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, CNN variants), reports hyperparameters, and follows evaluation standards expected by the community. Its focus on efficient, GPU-parallelizable models and thorough related-work positioning make it well targeted to the venue's audience. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—fast, accurate sequence labeling / NER—using a clearly described neural architecture (ID-CNN) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes 5.0) against appropriate baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, CNN variants). The methodology (dilated convolutions, iterative blocks, shared parameters), hyperparameter reporting, and segment-level micro-averaged F1 evaluation match the standards expected at top venues. The audience (NLP/ML researchers interested in efficient sequence models) and positioning relative to related work are appropriate, with limitations and comparisons clearly noted.

reviewer_a.justification: This is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a new sequence-labeling architecture (ID-CNN) and evaluates on standard NER benchmarks (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes 5.0) using segment-level micro-averaged F1. The paper compares against appropriate strong baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, CNN variants), reports hyperparameters, and follows evaluation standards expected by the community. Its focus on efficient, GPU-parallelizable models and thorough related-work positioning make it well targeted to the venue's audience.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—fast, accurate sequence labeling / NER—using a clearly described neural architecture (ID-CNN) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes 5.0) against appropriate baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, CNN variants). The methodology (dilated convolutions, iterative blocks, shared parameters), hyperparameter reporting, and segment-level micro-averaged F1 evaluation match the standards expected at top venues. The audience (NLP/ML researchers interested in efficient sequence models) and positioning relative to related work are appropriate, with limitations and comparisons clearly noted.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are generally supported: the architecture and parameter-sharing scheme are clearly described and experiments on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0 compare ID-CNNs against appropriate baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, matched receptive-field CNNs and ablations) showing competitive/superior segment-level F1 and faster, more parallelizable inference. There are no formal mathematical proofs needed beyond the architectural description, which is plausible and internally consistent (linear parameter growth, block iteration without extra parameters). The experimental methodology is sound in terms of baselines and ablations, though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or inclusion of character/lexicon features (which the authors note as a limitation), so minor concerns remain. Overall the conclusions are supported by the provided results, with only minor omissions that do not undermine the main technical claims. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clear and supported by experiments comparing ID-CNNs to strong baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, several CNN variants) on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0, showing competitive or superior segment-level micro-averaged F1. The technical description (iterated dilated convolution blocks with parameter sharing, linear parameter growth, and reported hyperparameters) is coherent and matches the presented empirical results; no complex mathematical proofs are required. Minor issues are that statistical significance testing is not mentioned and the authors exclude character/lexicon features (acknowledged), and greedy decoding limitations versus CRF are noted, so some experimental robustness details are lacking but do not overturn the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are generally supported: the architecture and parameter-sharing scheme are clearly described and experiments on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0 compare ID-CNNs against appropriate baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, matched receptive-field CNNs and ablations) showing competitive/superior segment-level F1 and faster, more parallelizable inference. There are no formal mathematical proofs needed beyond the architectural description, which is plausible and internally consistent (linear parameter growth, block iteration without extra parameters). The experimental methodology is sound in terms of baselines and ablations, though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or inclusion of character/lexicon features (which the authors note as a limitation), so minor concerns remain. Overall the conclusions are supported by the provided results, with only minor omissions that do not undermine the main technical claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clear and supported by experiments comparing ID-CNNs to strong baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, several CNN variants) on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0, showing competitive or superior segment-level micro-averaged F1. The technical description (iterated dilated convolution blocks with parameter sharing, linear parameter growth, and reported hyperparameters) is coherent and matches the presented empirical results; no complex mathematical proofs are required. Minor issues are that statistical significance testing is not mentioned and the authors exclude character/lexicon features (acknowledged), and greedy decoding limitations versus CRF are noted, so some experimental robustness details are lacking but do not overturn the main conclusions.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper targets an important practical problem—fast, token-level sequence labeling—by introducing ID-CNNs that are massively parallelizable on GPUs and achieve competitive or superior segment-level F1 versus strong baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF) on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0 while keeping parameter growth linear. Its combination of broad context via dilated convolutions and parameter-sharing iteration is likely to change practitioner choices where speed and parallelism matter and to spur follow-up work exploring dilated CNNs for structured prediction. The impact is moderated by stated limitations (no character/lexicon features included and greedy decoding trailing CRF on longer/diverse entities), so the contribution is strong but not outright transformative. | B(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—fast, accurate sequence labeling—and demonstrates an architecture (ID-CNN) that is massively parallelizable on GPUs and achieves competitive or superior segment-level F1 on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0 compared to strong Bi-LSTM baselines. By showing that stacked dilated convolutions with iterative, parameter-shared blocks can match accuracy while offering substantial speed/parallelism benefits, the work is likely to influence practitioner choices and spur follow-up work on efficient sequence models. The noted limitations (no character representations and greedy decoding trailing CRF on longer/more diverse entities) mean it is not purely transformative across all settings, but it nonetheless has strong impact and clear avenues for extension.

Its combination of broad context via dilated convolutions and parameter-sharing iteration is likely to change practitioner choices where speed and parallelism matter and to spur follow-up work exploring dilated CNNs for structured prediction. The impact is moderated by stated limitations (no character/lexicon features included and greedy decoding trailing CRF on longer/diverse entities), so the contribution is strong but not outright transformative.

By showing that stacked dilated convolutions with iterative, parameter-shared blocks can match accuracy while offering substantial speed/parallelism benefits, the work is likely to influence practitioner choices and spur follow-up work on efficient sequence models. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs experiments on two standard datasets (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes 5.0) and compares against strong baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, multiple CNN variants), which supports the main claims. Appropriate ablations/sensitivity checks are included (noshare variant, 1-loss vs intermediate-block losses, non-dilated CNN baselines), and related work coverage is reported as thorough. Limitations and failure modes are discussed (no character/lexicon features, greedy decoding weaker than CRF on longer/diverse entities), and hyperparameters are reported—but code is not mentioned and some deeper CNN variants were omitted for pragmatic reasons, leaving minor reproducibility and comparison gaps. | B(4): The paper evaluates ID-CNNs on two standard datasets (CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0) and compares to strong baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, greedy Bi-LSTM) while also including targeted ablations (non-dilated CNNs, noshare, 1-loss), which supports the main claims about accuracy and architectural choices. Related work appears to be covered thoroughly and several limitations are explicitly discussed (no character/lexicon features, greedy decoding weakness on OntoNotes). Hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, but code is not mentioned and the omission of character/lexicon experiments is a notable gap — overall a solid experimental evaluation with minor gaps, hence a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs experiments on two standard datasets (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes 5.0) and compares against strong baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, multiple CNN variants), which supports the main claims. Appropriate ablations/sensitivity checks are included (noshare variant, 1-loss vs intermediate-block losses, non-dilated CNN baselines), and related work coverage is reported as thorough. Limitations and failure modes are discussed (no character/lexicon features, greedy decoding weaker than CRF on longer/diverse entities), and hyperparameters are reported—but code is not mentioned and some deeper CNN variants were omitted for pragmatic reasons, leaving minor reproducibility and comparison gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates ID-CNNs on two standard datasets (CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0) and compares to strong baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, greedy Bi-LSTM) while also including targeted ablations (non-dilated CNNs, noshare, 1-loss), which supports the main claims about accuracy and architectural choices. Related work appears to be covered thoroughly and several limitations are explicitly discussed (no character/lexicon features, greedy decoding weakness on OntoNotes). Hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, but code is not mentioned and the omission of character/lexicon experiments is a notable gap — overall a solid experimental evaluation with minor gaps, hence a 4.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper applies iterated blocks of dilated convolutions with exponentially increasing dilation widths and parameter sharing (ID-CNN) to token-level sequence labeling, which is a notable architectural innovation compared to standard Bi-LSTMs, CRFs, and non-dilated CNNs. Sequence labeling itself is a well-studied problem, but the specific combination—iterating a dilated-convolution block to expand receptive field without extra parameters and emphasizing GPU parallelism for fast inference—is a clear, non-trivial contribution that is differentiated from prior baselines and produces competitive results on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0. Given these facts, the work represents a meaningful and informative novelty rather than a purely incremental tweak. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly specified ID-CNN architecture that uses stacks of dilated convolutions with exponentially increasing dilation widths and a novel iteration (parameter-sharing) of blocks to expand context without extra parameters—this is a distinct modeling idea compared to standard Bi-LSTMs and non-dilated CNNs. It differentiates itself via the iterated-block design and demonstrates competitive results on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0 against multiple baselines, indicating a non-trivial methodological advance. Overall, the contribution is a notable and informative novelty rather than a purely incremental recombination of known methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies iterated blocks of dilated convolutions with exponentially increasing dilation widths and parameter sharing (ID-CNN) to token-level sequence labeling, which is a notable architectural innovation compared to standard Bi-LSTMs, CRFs, and non-dilated CNNs. Sequence labeling itself is a well-studied problem, but the specific combination—iterating a dilated-convolution block to expand receptive field without extra parameters and emphasizing GPU parallelism for fast inference—is a clear, non-trivial contribution that is differentiated from prior baselines and produces competitive results on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0. Given these facts, the work represents a meaningful and informative novelty rather than a purely incremental tweak.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation to model description to experiments; the methodology is described concretely (dilated conv block B, Lc/Lb, shared params, losses, dropout). The main contributions are stated early, notation appears consistent, hyperparameters are reported, and related work is thorough. Writing quality is good and experimental comparisons (baselines, metrics) are presented clearly, though a few minor clarifications (e.g., elaborating the decoding trade-offs between greedy ID-CNN and CRF on OntoNotes) would improve readability. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from motivation to method (ID-CNN) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (architecture, dilation/iteration insight, speed/accuracy claims). Notation and model components are presented consistently (e.g., block B, Lc, Lb, iterative sharing) and hyperparameters and related work are reported thoroughly, which supports reproducibility and clarity. Writing quality is judged as good in the summary, but the provided notes do not detail figures/tables or illustrative examples, so I reserve a slight deduction for minor presentation uncertainties.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation to model description to experiments; the methodology is described concretely (dilated conv block B, Lc/Lb, shared params, losses, dropout). The main contributions are stated early, notation appears consistent, hyperparameters are reported, and related work is thorough. Writing quality is good and experimental comparisons (baselines, metrics) are presented clearly, though a few minor clarifications (e.g., elaborating the decoding trade-offs between greedy ID-CNN and CRF on OntoNotes) would improve readability.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper evaluates ID-CNN against strong, relevant baselines — Bi-LSTM-CRF, Bi-LSTM (greedy), and matched non-dilated CNNs (4- and 5-layer) as well as internal variants (noshare, 1-loss) — on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes using segment-level micro-averaged F1. The comparisons appear fair (same datasets and metrics), and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods do better (e.g., CRF/Viterbi decoding outperforms greedy decoding for long entities and shows a wider gap on OntoNotes) and note omitted features like character/lexicon inputs. They also analyze architectural differences (parameter sharing, receptive field, speed) and report thorough related-work coverage. | B(5): The paper compares against strong and relevant baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, greedy Bi-LSTM, multiple CNN variants including receptive-field-matched and noshare versions) on standard datasets (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes) using the same segment-level micro-averaged F1 metric. Comparisons appear fair (controlled baselines, matched receptive field, reported hyperparameters) and the paper acknowledges when CRF/Viterbi decoding outperforms greedy ID-CNNs for long/diverse entities. The authors also analyze differences (speed, parameter sharing, receptive field) and report related work thoroughly, so comparisons are comprehensive and well-motivated.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates ID-CNN against strong, relevant baselines — Bi-LSTM-CRF, Bi-LSTM (greedy), and matched non-dilated CNNs (4- and 5-layer) as well as internal variants (noshare, 1-loss) — on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes using segment-level micro-averaged F1. The comparisons appear fair (same datasets and metrics), and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods do better (e.g., CRF/Viterbi decoding outperforms greedy decoding for long entities and shows a wider gap on OntoNotes) and note omitted features like character/lexicon inputs. They also analyze architectural differences (parameter sharing, receptive field, speed) and report thorough related-work coverage.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against strong and relevant baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, greedy Bi-LSTM, multiple CNN variants including receptive-field-matched and noshare versions) on standard datasets (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes) using the same segment-level micro-averaged F1 metric. Comparisons appear fair (controlled baselines, matched receptive field, reported hyperparameters) and the paper acknowledges when CRF/Viterbi decoding outperforms greedy ID-CNNs for long/diverse entities. The authors also analyze differences (speed, parameter sharing, receptive field) and report related work thoroughly, so comparisons are comprehensive and well-motivated.

main_claims: Introduce ID-CNN: an architecture for sequence labeling that uses iterated blocks of dilated convolutions (ID-CNNs).
Stacking dilated convolutions with exponentially increasing dilation widths aggregates broad context while keeping parameter growth linear, and iterating blocks introduces no extra parameters.
ID-CNNs are massively parallelizable on GPUs and provide fast, token-level sequence labeling without down-/up-sampling.

ID-CNN achieves competitive or superior segment-level micro-averaged F1 compared to strong baselines (e.g., Bi-LSTM, greedy methods) on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes 5.0, and outperforms other greedy methods.
_summary.methodology: The model applies a dilation-1 convolution to inputs, then a stack of Lc-1 dilated convolution layers with exponentially increasing dilation widths to form a block B; B is iteratively applied Lb times (sharing parameters) and a final affine layer produces per-token class scores, trained with losses (including intermediate-block losses) and dropout.
baselines: noshare (ID-CNN variant without parameter sharing between blocks)
1-loss (variant computing loss only at final block)
limitations: The paper focuses on models using word embeddings alone and does not include character representations or lexicons (noted as likely beneficial future additions).
Greedy decoding (used by ID-CNN) is outperformed by CRF/Viterbi decoding for long entities and more diverse entity sets (wider gap on OntoNotes).
Deeper or more elaborate CNN architectures are not compared because they are slower, tend to over-fit on the relatively small dataset, and often require up-/down-sampling.
